core_concept: Economic Systems: Market Economy, Command Economy, and Mixed Economy
facts: A market economy answers the three economic questions—what to produce, how to produce it, and who gets it—through the price signals of supply and demand, not through government directives.

In a command economy, the government sets production targets and allocates resources through central planning, so a factory in a command economy makes what the plan says, not what customers want to buy.

Most modern countries have mixed economies because pure market economies can lead to inequality and pure command economies can cause shortages, so governments add regulation or public services to balance the two.
The Cold War was partly an ideological contest between the market economy of the United States and the command economy of the Soviet Union, not just a military standoff.

Private property is a core feature of capitalism because it allows individuals to own and trade resources for profit, whereas in socialism the government often owns major industries like steel or transportation.
A mixed economy uses both private property and government regulation; for example, the U.S. has private hospitals but also Medicare, while China has state-owned banks alongside private markets.
misconceptions: Students think a command economy means the government owns everything, but it actually means the government decides what to produce and how to distribute it, not that all property is government-owned.

Students believe a market economy has no rules at all, but even in capitalism the government enforces contracts, bans monopolies, and sets safety standards—that is why it is a mixed economy in practice.

Students think 'mixed economy' means half market and half command equally, but most countries lean more toward one system; for example, Sweden has heavy social services but still relies on private markets for most goods.
